skill.name: dogfood
skill.description: Systematically explore and test a web application to find bugs, UX issues, and other problems. Use when asked to "dogfood", "QA", "exploratory test", "find issues", "bug hunt", "test this app/site/platform", or review the quality of a web application. Produces a structured report with full reproduction evidence -- step-by-step screenshots, repro videos, and detailed repro steps for every issue -- so findings can be handed directly to the responsible teams.
skill.summary: Systematically explore a web application to find bugs, UX issues, and other problems, producing a structured report with full reproduction evidence.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: All 70 static findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate QA testing tool that uses the agent-browser CLI to explore web applications and document bugs. No malicious code execution, data exfiltration, or other security threats detected. The detected patterns are documentation of bash commands (not actual backtick execution), QA testing terminology (not system reconnaissance), and configurable parameters (not hardcoded secrets).

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm - False Positive
high_findings.description: Scanner incorrectly flagged text patterns like 'broken links', 'OTP', and template placeholders as weak cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic code exists in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations contain plain text documentation, not code. The scanner misinterprets words like 'broken', 'OTP', and template syntax as cryptographic references.

high_findings.title: Screen Capture Upload - False Positive
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged screenshot commands as 'screen capture upload'. Screenshots are saved to a local output directory for QA evidence, not uploaded to external servers.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill saves screenshots to a local directory (e.g., ./dogfood-output/screenshots/) for the user to review. There is no network upload functionality.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution - False Positive
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected backtick characters in markdown code blocks as shell execution. These are bash command examples in documentation, not actual code execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks appear within markdown fenced code blocks (```bash ... ```). These are documentation examples showing the syntax of agent-browser commands, not actual code execution.

medium_findings.title: Temp Directory Access - False Positive
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged /tmp/qa as temp directory access. This is a configurable output directory parameter, not a security issue.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill accepts an optional 'Output directory' parameter with default './dogfood-output/'. Users can specify any directory. This is standard testing behavior, not a vulnerability.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance - False Positive
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged QA testing terminology as system reconnaissance. Terms like 'explore pages', 'navigate', 'check console' are standard testing practices.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill performs exploratory QA testing - visiting pages, clicking buttons, checking for bugs. This is standard manual testing behavior, not malicious reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL / Python HTTP Libraries - False Positive
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged target URL parameter and agent-browser tool calls. The skill uses a CLI tool for browser automation, not Python HTTP libraries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The target URL is a user-provided parameter, not hardcoded. The skill uses 'agent-browser' CLI tool, not Python HTTP libraries. No data exfiltration detected.

content.user_title: Test Web Applications for Bugs and Issues
content.value_statement: Dogfood systematically explores web applications to find bugs, UX issues, and other problems, producing structured reports with full reproduction evidence including screenshots and videos.
actual_capabilities: Systematically explore a web application by visiting pages and testing features
Document bugs with step-by-step reproduction steps and evidence
Capture screenshots and record videos of issues for clear reproduction
Check browser console for JavaScript errors and failed requests
Test interactive elements like forms, buttons, and navigation flows
Produce a structured report with severity ratings for each issue
limitations: Cannot access or read the target application's source code
Cannot test non-web applications or APIs directly
Requires the agent-browser tool to be installed
Cannot perform load testing or stress testing

use_cases.title: QA Team Member Testing New Features
use_cases.description: A QA team member uses this skill to perform exploratory testing on a new feature before release, finding UI bugs and functional issues that automated tests missed.

use_cases.title: Developer Self-Testing Before Deployment
use_cases.description: A developer runs this skill against their localhost build to catch issues before submitting code, improving code quality and reducing regression bugs.

use_cases.title: Product Manager Reviewing UX
use_cases.description: A product manager uses the generated report to understand user experience issues and prioritize fixes for the development team.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Dogfood Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood {url} - explore the application and find bugs. Start with default settings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick exploratory test of a web application

prompt_templates.title: Focused Testing with Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood {url}. Focus on the checkout flow and payment forms. Find any issues that would block a user from completing a purchase.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing a specific feature or user flow

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood {url}. Sign in with credentials user@example.com and the password I provide. Test the user dashboard and settings pages.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing authenticated areas of an application

prompt_templates.title: Deep Exploratory Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood {url}. Output directory: /tmp/qa-report. Do a thorough exploration of the entire application. Aim to find 10+ well-documented issues with full reproduction steps and video evidence.

output_examples.output:
Report created at ./dogfood-output/report.md

Summary: 7 issues found
- Critical: 1 (Checkout button does nothing)
- High: 2 (Form validation accepts invalid email, Search returns no results)
- Medium: 3
- Low: 1

ISSUE-001: Checkout Button Non-Functional
- Severity: Critical
- Repro: Video at videos/issue-001-repro.webm
- Steps: 1. Navigate to /products 2. Click 'Add to Cart' 3. Click 'Checkout' 4. Observe: Page shows loading spinner indefinitely

output_examples.input: Dogfood http://example.com - Focus on the login page
output_examples.output:
Session started: example-com

Exploring login page...

ISSUE-001: No Error Message for Invalid Credentials
- Severity: Medium
- Screenshot: screenshots/issue-001.png
- Description: Entering invalid credentials shows no error message. User sees same login form with no feedback.

best_practices: Match evidence to issue type - use video for interactive bugs, screenshots for static issues like typos
Verify issues are reproducible before documenting - retry at least once to confirm
Write repro steps that map to screenshots so a reader can follow visually
anti_patterns: Recording video for static issues like typos or visible-on-load glitches
Exploring the entire app before documenting issues - document each issue as found
Reading the target app's source code instead of testing as a user

faq.question: What is dogfood testing?
faq.answer: Dogfood testing means using your own product like a real user would. It helps find bugs and UX issues that internal testing might miss.

faq.question: What tools does this skill use?
faq.answer: This skill uses the agent-browser CLI tool to automate browser interactions for testing web applications.

faq.question: How many issues should I aim to find?
faq.answer: Aim for 5-10 well-documented issues. Quality of evidence matters more than quantity - detailed reproduction steps are more valuable than a long list of vague issues.

faq.question: Can I test applications that require login?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide authentication credentials and the skill will sign in before testing. You can also save auth state for reuse.

faq.question: What evidence should I capture for each issue?
faq.answer: For interactive bugs (require user action), use video and step-by-step screenshots. For static issues (visible on load like typos or visual glitches), a single annotated screenshot is sufficient.

faq.question: Can I test only specific pages or features?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the Scope parameter to focus on specific areas like the checkout flow, billing page, or user dashboard.
